Torque arises from perpendicular force components relative to rotation axis.
Net force equals vector sum of all forces on system.
Work transfers energy via force over distance; conservative forces are path-independent.
Unbalanced forces produce a nonzero net force on a system.
Total kinetic energy combines translational and rotational motion components.
Translational kinetic energy equals one-half mass times velocity squared.
Gravitational force depends on mass and distance; acts along center-of-mass line.
Rotational kinetic energy depends on rotational inertia and angular velocity.
Matter states differ due to varying atomic and molecular interactions.
Centripetal acceleration magnitude equals speed squared divided by radius, directed center-ward.
Ideal springs exert force proportional to their length change from equilibrium.
Pressure equals perpendicular force divided by surface area.
Kinetic friction acts opposite to relative motion; independent of contact area.
Linear distance equals radius times angle for rotating points.
Scalars have magnitude only; vectors have magnitude and direction.
Angular displacement measures rotation angle in radians about specified axis.
Objects treated as point particles with mass and charge in models.
Net torque causes changes in angular velocity of objects or systems.
Elastic collision: total kinetic energy before equals total after.
SHM is a specific type of periodic motion with regular oscillations.
Center-of-mass velocity of object collections remains constant without external forces.
Motion is represented through diagrams, figures, graphs, equations, narratives.
Angular momentum of rigid systems equals moment of inertia times angular velocity.
Period and frequency are reciprocals; spring and pendulum oscillators have specific period equations.
SHM displacement equations show extrema and zeros; identifying these features describes motion.
Central object motion is negligible when satellite mass is much smaller.
Pressure differences drive fluid flow; mass flow rate conserves across tube sections.
Reference frame choice determines measured direction and magnitude of quantities.
Rotational equilibrium occurs when net torque is zero, independent of translational equilibrium.
Vectors decompose into perpendicular components that recombine as resultants.
Net force equals the rate of momentum change over time.
Linear momentum equals mass multiplied by velocity.
System properties arise from interactions between constituent objects.
Total system angular momentum equals sum of individual constituent parts.
Power measures the rate of energy change through transfer or conversion.
Total SHM energy equals kinetic energy plus potential energy.
Torque transfers energy when applied through angular displacement.
Forces are vectors describing interactions; always involve two distinct objects.
Rotational inertia measures resistance to rotation based on mass distribution.
Single-object systems contain only kinetic energy.
Newton's laws describe particle motion within fluids.
Forces between objects occur as equal and opposite paired interactions.
Systems with conservative forces between objects possess potential energy.
Work by torque depends on torque magnitude and angular displacement.
Choice of rotation axis affects the calculated angular momentum value.
Satellite orbital motion follows conservation of energy and momentum.
Angular momentum changes only through external interactions; systems conserved by design; nonrigid systems change speed without momentum change.
Rolling without slipping relates center of mass and rotational motion mathematically.
Vectors are represented as arrows with length proportional to magnitude.
Displacement quantifies change in position over time.
Three kinematic equations describe constant acceleration motion in one dimension.
Continuity equation expresses mass conservation in incompressible fluid flow.
Convert measurements between different reference frames.
Choose coordinate systems to resolve vectors into component form.
Systems can be modeled as single objects when individual properties are insignificant.
Fluid behavior results from particle interactions and external forces.
Contact forces result from touching; macroscopic effects of electric interactions.
Internal forces within systems do not affect center of mass motion.
Translational equilibrium occurs when net force on system is zero.
Acceleration magnitude is proportional to net force; direction matches net force.
Gravitational field strength equals force per unit mass; equals acceleration.
Pressure has magnitude only, no directional component.
Fluids lack fixed shape and conform to their containers.
Kinetic friction magnitude equals normal force times material-dependent coefficient.
Spring force magnitude follows Hooke's law relationship.
Multiple forces or force components can produce centripetal acceleration in varied contexts.
Kinetic energy has magnitude only, no directional component.
Work is a scalar with positive, negative, or zero values.
Average power equals total energy transferred divided by elapsed time.
Potential energy is a scalar quantity dependent on object positions.
Multi-object systems can have both kinetic and potential energy.
Total energy remains constant in a simple harmonic oscillator system.
Amplitude changes do not affect the period of SHM systems.
Momentum is a vector pointing in the velocity direction.
Impulse equals average force multiplied by time duration.
Restoring force proportional to displacement causes SHM; applies to small pendulum angles.
System momentum equals sum of individual object momenta.
Individual object kinetic energies change; system total remains constant.
Average angular velocity is angular position change per unit time.
Linear velocity and tangential acceleration relate to angular quantities by radius.
Lever arm measures perpendicular distance from axis to force line.
Rotational inertia for point mass equals mass times distance squared.
Unbalanced torques cause angular velocity to change; balanced torques maintain constant angular velocity.
Angular acceleration is directly proportional to net torque and inversely proportional to rotational inertia.
A system can rotate with kinetic energy even when its center of mass is stationary.
Total rotational inertia is the sum of individual rotational inertias.
Tension results from internal forces in strings; ideal strings have uniform tension throughout.
Systems can exchange energy and mass with their environment.
Average power equals total work done divided by time interval.
Momentum analyzes collisions where internal forces dominate and explosions where internal forces separate objects.
Use trigonometry and Pythagorean theorem to find vector components.
Ideal rolling without slipping conserves energy; friction dissipates nothing.
Impulse is a vector pointing in direction of net force.
Observed velocity combines object and observer velocities; acceleration is frame-invariant.
Earth's gravitational acceleration is downward, constant, approximately 10 m/s².
Potential energy differences create kinetic energy and pressure differences via conservation.
Rotational kinetic energy is a scalar, not a vector quantity.
Internal momentum changes balance; system momentum changes equal external impulse.
Average velocity and acceleration depend on initial and final states.
Circular orbits conserve total energy, potential energy, angular momentum, and kinetic energy.
Inelastic collision: total system kinetic energy decreases.
Distance and speed are scalars; position, displacement, velocity, and acceleration are vectors.
Straight-line angular momentum depends on distance, mass, speed, and angle.
Average angular acceleration is angular velocity change per unit time.
Fluid density equals mass divided by volume.
Static friction acts between non-sliding surfaces in contact.
Incompressible fluids maintain constant volume and density under pressure.
Rotating rigid systems may require independent linear and rotational analysis simultaneously.
Spring force always points toward the equilibrium position.
All points on rigid body share identical angular velocity and acceleration.
Tangential acceleration measures speed change along the circular path direction.
Weight is gravitational force on object near astronomical body.
Velocity changes only when a nonzero net external force acts.
Kinetic energy values vary depending on the observer's reference frame.
Only force component parallel to displacement changes system energy.
Force diagrams show forces' magnitudes, directions, and application locations.
Zero potential energy reference point is chosen by observer for convenience.
Maximum kinetic energy occurs when potential energy reaches its minimum.
Free-body diagrams visualize forces and help determine governing equations.
Angular momentum is universally conserved across all physical interactions.
Mechanical energy equals kinetic plus potential energy.
Buoyant force is an upward force a fluid exerts on objects.
Torque-angle graph area represents work done on system.
Graphical representations reveal and analyze SHM system properties.
Zero net force means constant velocity of system.
Torque magnitude depends on force, distance, and angle between vectors.
Ideal fluids cannot be compressed and have no internal friction.
Elastic potential energy depends on spring displacement from equilibrium.
Free-body diagrams show all external forces acting on an object.
Maximum potential energy occurs at minimum kinetic energy; amplitude changes alter total energy.
Individual objects within systems behave differently from the whole system.
Net acceleration combines centripetal and tangential acceleration components vectorially.
Conservation of momentum determines velocities before and after collisions.
Bernoulli's equation expresses mechanical energy conservation in fluid flow.
Energy changes within systems must balance internally or transfer externally.
Average velocity equals displacement divided by time interval.
Elliptical orbits conserve total energy and angular momentum; potential and kinetic energy vary.
Buoyant force results from collective forces of fluid particles.
Static friction adjusts to prevent sliding up to maximum limit; typically exceeds kinetic.
Gravitational force is constant when change in position is negligible.
Velocity changes only in direction of unbalanced force.
Lost kinetic energy converts to heat, sound, deformation via nonconservative forces.
Instantaneous power relates force component parallel to velocity.
Analyze 2D motion by applying 1D kinematics to separated components.
Rotational inertia is minimum when axis passes through center of mass.
Vector sums in one dimension use opposite signs for opposite directions.
Angular impulse equals torque multiplied by time interval.
Fluid pressure results from particle collisions with surfaces.
Zero net external torque means constant total angular momentum.
Impulse equals area under force-versus-time graph curve.
Net work equals change in kinetic energy; friction dissipates energy over path.
Instantaneous velocity equals the slope of position-time graph tangent line.
Rotational kinematics parallels linear kinematics with same mathematical relationships.
Rolling with slipping decouples translational and rotational motions.
Torricelli's theorem links exit velocity to height difference using energy conservation.
System internal structure affects how the system is analyzed.
Average acceleration equals change in velocity divided by time.
Instantaneous acceleration equals the slope of velocity-time graph tangent line.
Projectile motion combines zero acceleration horizontally with constant vertical acceleration.
Force vectors originate from center of mass; mass concentrated there.
Inertial reference frame verifies Newton's first law.
Earth's surface gravitational field strength approximates ten newtons per kilogram.
Period and frequency describe uniform circular motion; period equals one over frequency.
Work equals area under force-versus-displacement graph.
Gravitational potential energy between masses follows inverse distance relationship.
Proper system selection can make total energy constant.
Net force equals slope of momentum-versus-time graph.
Momentum conserves in all physical interactions.
Perfectly inelastic: objects combine and move with single final velocity.
Parallel axis theorem relates inertia for parallel axes using distance offset.
Angular impulse direction matches the applied torque direction.
Nonzero net external torque transfers angular momentum to environment.
Slipping friction dissipates energy as its point of application moves.
Gravitational potential energy is zero at infinite separation between objects.
Absolute pressure equals reference pressure plus gauge pressure.
Buoyant force equals the weight of displaced fluid.
Angular impulse equals area under torque-versus-time graph curve.
Energy change in system equals energy transferred in or out.
Zero net external force means total system momentum remains constant.
Near planetary surface, gravitational potential energy change approximated linearly.
Orbital centripetal acceleration comes from gravity; period and radius depend on central mass.
Displacement equals the area under a velocity-time graph curve.
Acceleration occurs when velocity magnitude or direction changes.
External variable changes can alter a system's internal substructure.
Gauge pressure in fluid column depends on density, gravity, height.
Escape velocity occurs when system's total mechanical energy equals zero.
Change in momentum is final momentum minus initial momentum.
Align coordinate axes with acceleration direction for simpler equations.
Apparent weight equals magnitude of normal force on system.
Change in angular momentum equals final minus initial angular momentum.
At escape velocity, satellite velocity approaches zero at infinite distance.
Nonzero external force transfers momentum between system and environment.
Impulse-momentum theorem connects impulse and momentum change.
Acceleration causes apparent weight to differ from gravitational force.
Instantaneous values approach averages as time intervals shrink.
Energy conservation holds universally in all interactions.
Total system potential energy is sum of all pairwise potential energies.
Change in velocity equals the area under an acceleration-time graph.
Center of mass lies on symmetry lines for symmetrical mass distributions.
Angular impulse equals change in angular momentum of object.
Escape velocity derives from energy conservation: vesc = √(2GM/r).
Newton's second law derives from impulse-momentum theorem.
Zero net work and no nonconservative forces preserve mechanical energy.
Weightlessness occurs with no forces or gravity alone.
Center of mass position calculated using weighted average mass distribution formula.
Nonzero net work causes energy transfer with surroundings.
Noninertial observers cannot distinguish apparent weight from gravitational force.
Rotational impulse-momentum theorem follows from rotational Newton's second law.
Systems can be modeled as single objects located at center of mass.
Net torque equals slope of angular momentum versus time graph.
Inertial mass measures resistance to motion changes.
Gravitational mass determines force of attraction between systems.
Inertial and gravitational mass are experimentally proven equivalent.
